name: ScyllaDB — 3-node cluster, caching workload (uniform), YCSB (benchANT 2023)
notes: : the second MEASURED latency-at-load corpus entry, the paired half of the MongoDB Atlas entry from the SAME benchANT study (github.com/benchANT/mongodb-vs-scylladb), 'caching' workload, uniform key distribution, configuration 'scylladb-small-caching-90-kops' — ScyllaDB 5.1.3, 3x i4i.2xlarge (AWS us-west-2), replication factor 3, consistency QUORUM (read+write), driven by 2 YCSB CoreWorkload loader nodes each issuing '-target 45000' (90,000 op/s nominal combined), 50/50 read/update, uniform distribution, ~1 KB records (fieldCount 10 x fieldLength 100), 1800 s measurement window. RE-VERIFIED directly from the raw per-run YCSB output files (0_run.txt, 1_run.txt): summing [OVERALL] Throughput(ops/sec) gives a combined 77,176.88 op/s (85.75% of the 90,000 nominal target); averaging [READ] AverageLatency(us) gives 4,203.08 us (4.2031 ms), and [UPDATE] AverageLatency(us) gives 2,262.53 us (2.2625 ms) — both match the figures this task was briefed with, and the combined throughput independently matches the ScyllaDB engineering blog's own narrative for this exact configuration ('the small ScyllaDB cluster is able to serve 77 kOps/s with a cluster utilization of ~87%'). MAPPING CHOICE: SDA has no dedicated ScyllaDB component; ScyllaDB is CQL-wire-compatible with Apache Cassandra, and the corpus already establishes db.cassandra as the mapping for a CQL-protocol store's aggregate cluster capacity (the 2017 ScyllaDB-vs-Cassandra write-ceiling entry uses the SAME db.cassandra type for a benchmark measuring Cassandra ITSELF, published by ScyllaDB — the wire/keyspace-model compatibility, not the vendor, is what db.cassandra represents here). HONEST METRIC CHOICE: 50/50 read/update workload, so the scored ground truth is the BLENDED per-operation mean — (READ_mean + UPDATE_mean)/2 = (4.2031 + 2.2625)/2 = 3.2328 ms — SDA's single-service-time-per-node simplification; the per-op split (4.20 ms read vs 2.26 ms update, ScyllaDB's reads costing MORE than updates — the opposite asymmetry direction from the MongoDB entry) is recorded as context, not separately scored. UNDERDETERMINATION GUARD: PIN the pool from a sourced fact scaled by this cluster's OWN documented topology, FIT the held time — see the connectionPool tunable note. The actual `pnpm calibrate` fit lands at connectionHeldMs = 2.45638 ms, rho = 0.9874 (< 1, verified) — post-fit residual +1.1% (grid-search granularity, see CALIBRATION-REPORT.md) — a physically sensible held time (a couple of milliseconds), consistent with the blog's own '~87% utilization' figure for this exact cluster. COUPLING DECISION: this entry's tunables are NODE-scoped (selector.node = 'scylla'), an INDEPENDENT judgment call from the existing 2017 Cassandra write-ceiling entry, which pins connectionPool=64 UNSCALED (a documented, acknowledged simplification in that entry's own note) and only fits a type-scoped db.cassandra:throughput — a DIFFERENT deployment (Apache Cassandra 3.0.9, 2017, i2.8xlarge instances, cassandra-stress) from this one (ScyllaDB 5.1.3, 2023, i4i.2xlarge, YCSB). Declaring this entry's connectionPool/connectionHeldMs as type-scoped db.cassandra tunables would NOT directly collide with the 2017 entry's tunable (it declares no connectionHeldMs tunable, and its throughput tunable is a different key) — but node-scoping keeps this entry's own model-level pin/fit fully independent of that different deployment regardless, matching the corpus's own per-model-override mechanism (the same one the 2017 entry itself uses for its static pool+held in cassandra-cluster.sda.json) and leaving room for a future db.cassandra-family point to share deliberately, never by accident. SCOPE: single scored point for this exact metric (no leave-one-out partner yet — the honest disjoint fallback applies); config-sensitive (one specific ScyllaDB tier/instance family/workload mix).

sources.note: The 2 raw YCSB run outputs (0_run.txt, 1_run.txt) for the 'scylladb-small-caching-90-kops' configuration — each carries [OVERALL] Throughput(ops/sec), [READ] AverageLatency(us), [UPDATE] AverageLatency(us). Re-verified directly: summed throughput 77,176.88 op/s; simple-average READ 4,203.08 us; simple-average UPDATE 2,262.53 us (op counts ~34.7M per run per type, near-equal, so the simple average is a faithful stand-in for the op-weighted mean).

The benchmark configuration: ScyllaDB 5.1.3, dbaasProvider SCYLLADB, numberOfNodes 3, replicationFactor 3, instanceFlavor i4i.2xlarge, region us-west-2, clientConfiguration {writeConsistency: QUORUM, readConsistency: QUORUM}; benchmarkNodes.numberOfNodes 2; YCSB CoreWorkload targetOps 90000, readProportion 0.5, updateProportion 0.5, requestdistribution UNIFORM, fieldCount 10, fieldLength 100, maxExecutionTime 1800.

ScyllaDB engineering blog's own deep-dive on this exact caching benchmark pairing: 'the small ScyllaDB cluster is able to serve 77 kOps/s with a cluster utilization of ~87%' — independently matches this entry's re-verified 77,176.9 op/s and corroborates a fitted rho close to (but below) 1.
benchANT's own summary blog for the same study (narrative cross-check only; no additional numbers beyond the raw results used here).

tunables.note: PINNED, not fitted: cassandra.yaml's documented per-node concurrent_reads + concurrent_writes defaults (32+32=64, CASSANDRA_POOL_SOURCE — the same source db.cassandra's catalog default already cites) x 3 nodes (this cluster's own documented numberOfNodes, evaluationScenario.json). An INDEPENDENT scaling choice from the 2017 Cassandra write-ceiling entry (which pins 64 UNSCALED for its own 3-node cluster, an acknowledged simplification in that entry's own note) — this entry reasons from the cluster size directly. Capacity = pool/held is degenerate along the ratio, so the sourced structural fact is pinned and connectionHeldMs is fitted. NODE-scoped (selector.node, not selector.type) so this pin never couples with the 2017 entry or any other db.cassandra entry.

FITTED: the per-operation connection-hold time that, at the pinned 192-slot pool and the achieved 77,176.9 op/s load, reproduces the measured 3.2328 ms blended read/update mean via the M/M/c sojourn (Erlang-C). Range capped at 2.48 ms — just below the pool's own saturation cliff (192 slots / 77,176.9 op/s = 2.488 ms, where rho would reach 1) so the search never wanders into an infeasible (rho>=1) region. NODE-scoped (selector.node = 'scylla'), independent of the 2017 Cassandra write-ceiling entry's tunables (a different deployment: Apache Cassandra 3.0.9 vs ScyllaDB 5.1.3, different hardware, different era, different benchmark tool).

groundTruth.note: The BLENDED mean latency (READ 4.2031 ms + UPDATE 2.2625 ms, averaged — the workload is exactly 50/50) at the achieved combined load of 77,176.9 op/s (85.75% of the 90,000 nominal target), re-verified from the 2 raw YCSB run files. SDA's out-of-box estimate (catalog default connectionHeldMs 5 ms on a 64-slot pool) predicts a saturated or near-idle sojourn far from the measured value; the fit corrects the pool (pinned, sourced, scaled by cluster size) and the hold time (fitted) to reproduce it, landing at rho ~= 0.987 — a real, verified-sub-1 utilization consistent with the blog's own '~87% utilization' figure for this cluster. The per-op split (4.20 ms read vs 2.26 ms update — reads costing MORE than updates, the opposite direction from the MongoDB entry) is NOT separately scored: one node, one service-time distribution, the honest residual.
